quizzes.lessonTitle: Producing a clear, defensible assessment report
quizzes.title: Assessing Indoor Environment Conditions — Knowledge Check

questions.questionText: What best describes indoor environmental quality (IEQ) as covered in this course?
options: Only whether a building smells clean
The condition of a building’s air, surfaces and moisture balance as they affect the building and its occupants
A fixed pass/fail score set by the manufacturer of the moisture meter
A measure that only applies to commercial buildings, not homes

questions.questionText: What is the correct order of the assessor’s core role?
options: Recommend, then measure, then observe
Guess, then confirm with a single reading
Observe, measure, interpret, recommend
Measure only, since observation is subjective

questions.questionText: Why should an assessor view a building as a system rather than assessing the wet patch alone?
options: Because moisture, ventilation, HVAC and occupancy all interact to shape drying and mould risk
Because building system checks are required only for commercial insurance claims
Because HVAC systems never affect areas outside the room where they are installed
Because occupancy has no measurable effect on indoor moisture

questions.questionText: Why is specific humidity (g/kg) useful for comparing two rooms at different temperatures, when relative humidity (RH) alone is not?
options: Specific humidity is measured in the United States and is therefore more standardised
Specific humidity records the actual mass of water in the air, so it does not change just because temperature changes
RH is always a more precise reading than specific humidity
Specific humidity replaces the need to record temperature at all

questions.questionText: When comparing a material moisture reading in an affected room, what should it be compared against?
options: A generic published number for that material type
An unaffected reference reading of the same material, taken on the same day with the same instrument
The previous tenant’s reported comfort level
The reading taken outdoors

questions.questionText: A hygrometer shows moderate relative humidity in a room, but a nearby window is visibly sweating. What does this indicate?
options: The instrument is faulty and should be discarded
The room is fully dry and no further action is needed
The dew point is close to the window’s surface temperature, creating a condensation risk despite the moderate RH reading
Condensation on glass has no relationship to dew point

questions.questionText: What is the correct approach when mould is present or strongly suspected during an inspection?
options: Run air movers across the affected surfaces immediately to dry them out faster
Use air filtration devices (AFDs) and dehumidifiers to manage moisture and airborne spores, and introduce air movers only after the area is cleaned and cleared
Ignore it unless the occupant specifically raises a health complaint
Ventilate the area briefly and then proceed as normal with standard drying equipment

questions.questionText: During an inspection, a technician suspects a wall sheet installed before the mid-1980s may contain asbestos. What is the correct action?
options: Sand a small section to confirm visually before deciding
Continue the assessment as normal since it is only a suspicion, not a confirmed finding
Stop, do not disturb the material, secure the area, and refer to a licensed asbestos assessor
Remove the material carefully by hand and dispose of it with general waste

questions.questionText: Why does Australian climate zone and season matter when interpreting a moisture or humidity reading?
options: It does not matter; a single national baseline applies everywhere
Because a reading considered normal in a tropical wet season may look elevated in an arid region in winter, and vice versa, so the reference must reflect local, current conditions
Because climate zone only affects outdoor readings, never indoor ones
Because instruments read differently depending on the manufacturer’s country of origin

questions.questionText: What makes an indoor environment assessment report defensible in an Australian insurance or dispute context?
options: Confident, authoritative language regardless of the underlying evidence
A visible, checkable trail from observations and measurements, through reference comparison and interpretation, to a proportionate recommendation and stated limitations
Omitting any findings that could be seen as uncertain or inconclusive
A single overall pass/fail rating with no supporting detail
